name: Computer Fraud and Abuse Act
description: US federal computer crime statute codified at 18 U.S.C. §1030. Criminalizes a range of unauthorized access offenses: accessing computers without authorization, exceeding authorized access, computer fraud, damaging protected computers, trafficking in passwords, threatening to damage computers, etc. Originally enacted 1986; amended significantly by USA PATRIOT Act 2001, Identity Theft Enforcement Act 2008, Cyber Intelligence Sharing and Protection Act 2015, and others.
relationToCloudSecurity: CFAA applies to unauthorized access into cloud-hosted systems. Security researchers, penetration testers, and red teams must operate under explicit authorization to avoid CFAA exposure. 2021 Supreme Court ruling (Van Buren v. United States) narrowed 'exceeds authorized access' interpretation.
relationToAISecurity: Adversarial AI / model-evasion research that involves accessing production AI systems without authorization can implicate CFAA. Recent DOJ guidance (2022) clarifies good-faith security research safe harbor.

license.notes: US federal statutes are public-domain government works (17 USC §105) and may be freely redistributed.

lifecycle.notes: Continuously amended. Current text at 18 USC §1030.

current_extraction.notes: Metadata-only stub. Source content acquisition + structured extraction is TODO.
